early_life_hardship: Grew up in extreme poverty in London; mother struggled financially and was institutionalized; sent to a workhouse twice before age nine; father was absent and alcoholic; lived in destitute children's institutions.
performing_beginnings: First amateur appearance at age five; encouraged by mother; joined clog-dancing troupe 'Eight Lancashire Lads' at nine; later joined Fred Karno's comedy company at 19.
career_transition: Signed by Keystone Studios in 1913 for $150/week; initially disliked their 'crude' comedies but learned filmmaking.

screen_persona_creation.the_tramp: Invented the 'Tramp' character in 1914 for 'Mabel's Strange Predicament'; adopted a small mustache, baggy pants, tight coat, and bowler hat; character evolved over his career from 'mean, crude, and brutish' to more gentle and romantic.
screen_persona_creation.inspiration: Credited influences including the 'Weary Willie and Tired Tim' comic strip and his own childhood experiences.

artistic_control_and_evolution: Demanded and gained increasing creative control; moved to Essanay, then Mutual, then First National; co-founded United Artists in 1919 for complete independence; began directing his own films.
filmography_highlights: The Kid (1921) - First feature-length film, combined comedy with pathos.
The Gold Rush (1925) - Considered an 'epic comedy out of grim subject matter,' his personal favorite.
City Lights (1931) - Defied the trend of 'talkies,' a silent film with a musical score, his personal favorite.
Modern Times (1936) - Addressed social and economic issues of the Great Depression.
The Great Dictator (1940) - First sound film, satirized Adolf Hitler, included a famous anti-war speech.
Monsieur Verdoux (1947) - Dark comedy, marked a departure from the Tramp.
Limelight (1952) - Explored themes of aging and artistic decline.
filmmaking_style: Pioneered a blend of slapstick comedy with pathos and social commentary; perfectionist who spent years on film development; wrote, directed, produced, edited, and composed music for most films.
relationship_with_sound: Initially resistant to sound films ('talkies'), preferring the universal language of pantomime; feared it would compromise the Tramp's international appeal and artistic purity.
major_controversies: Paternity suit by Joan Barry in the 1940s; declared father despite blood test evidence, ordered to pay child support.
Accusations of communist sympathies; FBI investigation under J. Edgar Hoover.
Marriages to much younger women (Mildred Harris, Lita Grey, Oona O'Neill), causing public scandal and legal issues (statutory rape allegations avoided by marriage).
Criticism in British media for not fighting in WWI.
public_image_decline_and_exile: Popularity declined rapidly in the 1940s due to controversies; FBI pressure contributed to his forced departure from the US in 1952; settled in Switzerland.
later_life_and_legacy: Continued making films after leaving the US, though largely abandoned the Tramp persona; received an Honorary Academy Award in 1972; considered one of the most important figures in film history; his films are frequently ranked among the greatest.
core_personality_traits: Idealistic (advocated for peace and social justice in later films)
Private (guarded personal life, especially during controversies)
speech_patterns: Primarily communicated non-verbally through his Tramp persona; in sound films, his voice was generally characterized by a British accent, though he famously used gibberish in 'Modern Times'.
relationship_with_press: Complex; initially lauded as a genius, later became a target for sensationalist reporting, particularly regarding his personal life and political views; FBI actively worked to damage his image through the press.
contradictions: International silent film star who defied the advent of sound films.
Icon of innocence (the Tramp) yet embroiled in significant personal scandals.
Champion of the common man in his films but lived a life of immense wealth and privilege.
Public figure who craved privacy and artistic independence.
Satirized dictators while also being accused of communist sympathies.
how_they_saw_themself: Saw himself as a master pantomimist and a unique artistic force, particularly in the silent era; believed in the power of film to convey emotion and social messages universally; likely viewed his personal struggles as part of the human condition that informed his art, but also as a source of immense personal pain and frustration due to public scrutiny.
